Vauban thinks owning a car is a pain and forgets about car-related stress. More than half the people there got rid of their cars before moving and don't miss them at all. Andrew Selsky, in "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," shares Carlos Arturo Plaza's thoughts. As he biked with his wife, he said, "It's super relaxing!"

Plus, Plaza added, "The air is way cleaner, which is great." More and more people see how much cars pollute. Paris, known for its smog, started a plan to clean up the air. Robert Duffer, in "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city," explained that cars with even numbers were banned some days, and odd numbers on others. The result? Huge! Duffer wrote, "Traffic dropped 60 percent in Paris after five really smoggy days." Elizabeth Rosenthal, in "The End of Car Culture," says, "If this keeps up...the planet will be better off, since cars cause so much pollution in the US." We Americans buy too much stuff, leading to lots of pollution. We want the newest gadgets, which costs more than just money. Greenhouse gasses are increasing, so we need to change. Rosenthal mentions that "President Obama set some big goals to cut America's greenhouse gas emissions last week." In car-free places, it's not just about the air. Andrew Selsky wrote about "the construction of 118 miles of bicycle paths" and "rough sidewalks swapped for smooth ones" in Bogota, Colombia. This makes walking more appealing. The writer shares that "officials from other countries came to Bogota to see carfree day and were pumped," including the mayor of Paraguay's capital. Seeing good changes makes others want to join in. Columbia's event has sparked talks nearby, with "two other Colombian cities, Calli and Valledupar, joined in." Even so, car companies are growing. Rosenthal explains, "But it could hurt the car business. So, companies like Ford and Mercedes are calling themselves "mobility" companies that do more than just make cars." Since walking, biking, and buses are becoming popular, car companies are trying to keep customers, even without cars. She mentions Bill Ford's idea of car dealerships "work with phone companies to build cities where 'walking, bikes, cars, and buses all work together to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer.'" Combining transit with tech for everyone's benefit.

No doubt about it, things are changing. Cities are trying new ways to get around or improving the old ones. Henry Ford might be sad that cars are less popular, but nature is happy about our eco-friendly efforts. The future is still unwritten, but we only have one Earth, so let's take care of it.

The uproar regarding eliminating the Electoral College feels urgent, reminiscent of the 2000 election drama. Many view it as unfair, unneeded, and ready to be scrapped.

But wait, there's another side! Richard A. Posner argued it stops "elections where no one gets a clear majority." It gives us a definite winner, dodging long and messy runoff elections. Posner also noted the winner usually gets way more Electoral College votes than popular votes, making big fights over the result less likely than if we just counted individual votes. Still, the basic idea is: people should pick their president. So, the Electoral College looks messed up and confusing, which makes people want to get rid of it.

A big worry is that the person most people voted for might not win the presidency, meaning the country doesn't get who it really wants. Bradford Plumer says the Electoral College means "the popular vote winner [could lose] again." This takes away people's power, which is bad for democracy. Plumer also mentions that voters can't always "control how their electors vote," so they have less say in who wins. This hurts the idea of people ruling, so it's a strong reason to get rid of the Electoral College now.

Another issue is how much power electors have. Plumer points out that "electors could always go against what the people want," showing how much they matter in picking the president. This power should belong to us, the citizens! He explains how electors "have sometimes voted against their party and picked whoever they wanted," showing they can do their own thing. Popular vote is the only thing that matters. America should go where its people want, and that means directly electing the president.

To sum it up, the Electoral College is an unfair setup for Americans. The right to choose our leader must be protected. The popular vote should win, and we should say goodbye to the Electoral College for good.

People drive to work or school every day. But things are changing! More people are driving less, which is great for everyone. It helps the Earth and makes us less stressed.

Driving less means less pollution. That's good for the environment! Car exhaust makes smog, which traps bad stuff in the air. Cities often have smog because of this. For example, Paris had 147 micrograms of air pollution once. After they limited cars, the smog disappeared fast – in just one day, and it didn't come back! Cars make a lot of greenhouse gases: 12% in Europe and 50% in the US. This shows how bad cars are for the environment. Even one day a week without cars can really lower air pollution. Even Americans are changing! Fewer people are buying or driving cars. Since '95, people drive almost 9% less. If we keep driving less, we can lower pollution and help the environment a lot.

Besides helping the environment, not driving every day makes us less stressed. In Vauban, Germany, not many people own cars – only 30%. Even though some people think families need minivans, Heidrun Walter likes not having a car. She said, "When I had a car, I was really stressed. Now I am much happier and more relaxed." For three years, Bogota, Colombia, has had a "Day Without Cars." Only taxis and buses are allowed. Even when it rains, people love this day. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, also said, "It's a great way to relax and clean up the air for everyone." Now two other cities in Colombia want to do the same thing. Also, technology lets us work from home, talk to friends online, and avoid driving. This can really lower stress!

We used to think cars were the best way to get around. But, we didn't always think about how they hurt the Earth or our own health. It's time to think about what we do and learn more.

So, the Electoral College, right? It's a truly awful method for selecting our president, if you ask me! It really does a disservice to the voters. Electors from various states sometimes just decide to go their own way and vote as they please! Do you remember Al Gore? He actually secured the most individual votes but was somehow still defeated. No way! This whole system is like, super old.

When casting your ballot, what you're really doing is picking "electors." They're supposed to vote for whomever wins, but honestly, they can do whatever they want! Someone could win the total vote of every individual, but still not win the election! How does that even happen? Because they don't get enough electoral votes from each state, that's how. Seriously unfair! Whoever the people pick should win, plain and simple. Electors used to be important because they were supposed to choose wisely for all the dummies, but now? Anybody can pick a decent leader. Gore lost way back in 2000, even though he won the most votes. Proof positive that this system stinks!

But why is the Electoral College so terrible, really? It's this "winner-take-all" thing. You win a state, even if it's just barely, and boom, you get ALL the votes for that state. Candidates totally ignore smaller states, or states they already know they'll win. Instead, they just focus on big swing states, like Ohio, the state that always changes its mind. Is that smart? Nope! Back in 2000, a whole 17 states were just completely ignored! Voters forgotten, just clueless. In races that are super close, half the voters don't even count. Take California, for instance; almost half of the 35 million voters there don't even matter! Elections shouldn't just depend on Ohio when everyone else gets ignored.

There is a need for a better system, plain and simple. Only a popular vote on a national level can promise that the person most voters wanted gets the job. Candidates would be forced to actually listen to everyone, even people living in rural areas! American citizens deserve to have fair elections that make the most people happy.

In the U.S., we elect presidents using the Electoral College, a system the founders made. When you vote, you pick electors who then vote for president and vice president. Congress counts all these votes. Supporters think it ensures a clear winner. But others disagree, saying it favors big states and can cause weird outcomes. The winner-take-all idea feels unfair.

A state's electors match its Congress members, so bigger states have more say. Hawaii has just four electors, while Texas has thirty-eight! This makes candidates focus on big cities. Bradford Plumer said Wyoming has "one vote for 500,000 voters, the same power as 55 people in California with 35 million voters. Congress's pick might not be what the people really want."

Our way of choosing presidents is complex. In 2000, Gore won more votes but lost. Can we trust electors to keep their promises? An elector changing their vote could change the election. Plus, Hawaii had two sets of electors in 1960.

A big problem is the winner-take-all rule. Getting the most votes in a state means getting all its electors. So, candidates ignore states they think they can't win and focus on "swing states." Richard A. Posner noted, "In 2000, 17 states saw no candidates, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in 25 big media markets saw no ads."

So, the Electoral College has issues. It causes problems and seems unfair. Even Nixon and Carter wanted to get rid of it. Candidates spend too much time in some states, electors might break promises, and the winner-take-all rule is unfair. The Electoral College makes things harder.

Wow, this feels freeing! Would you believe it? My ride is *history*! Okay, so cars are everywhere. And admit it, most folks crave one, yeah? Giving up a car? Sounds daunting. But hey, maybe it ain’t all doom and gloom! Maybe the times, they are a-changin’? Perhaps this could actually be a good turn!

Ever heard of Vauban, Germany? Folks *dig* living there sans cars! Hard to believe, but almost 70% of families there? Car-free! Get this—more than half, like 57%, *dumped* their cars just to relocate there! Alright, it's mainly Vauban for now, but hey, it's spreading. Elisabeth Rosenthal says those cars pump out a massive 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases. But in car-crazy spots like the U.S.? That could skyrocket to 50%! Robert Duffer noted Paris cleaned up its act fast by axing cars. Less driving, less yuck in the air, a happier planet – rad, right? Picture more Vaubans popping up!

Even Bogota, Colombia, has a special day that makes it a blast to ditch the car! That day? Folks just hoof it, bike it, skate it, or hop on a bus. The mayor's been at it for three years, rain or shine! Other Colombian cities, like Cali and Valledupar, are jumping on the bandwagon. This could blow up! Maybe it'll even become a national day. And fewer cars mean more walking, which might get governments to spruce up those sidewalks and paths. We might even see snazzy new restaurants, parks, and shops for walkers, which would improve our lives! If everyone went car-free, we'd unearth fresh and fun ways to spend our time, and profit from fewer car trips.

Elisabeth Rosenthal thinks we might even ease off the gas pedal in the U.S. soon. Numbers show fewer folks are buying cars, hitting the road, or snagging licenses lately. Since the U.S. is so pivotal, and car use is waning, governments could give things a nudge. They could tweak things to make cars less of a must-have. Then, we might just find ourselves gabbing on the bus, making new connections! Less driving might even lead to friendships!

From sweet, clean air to a way better way of life, and yeah, even pals, cutting down on driving boasts heaps of awesome perks!

It seems these days, fewer people are driving. Instead, lots of people are sharing rides or trying out new ways of getting around. For example, Elisabeth Rosenthal talks about Vauban in Germany, where cars are pretty much not allowed, which really cuts down on driving. Robert Duffer also points out the big pollution problems in Paris and the cool solutions they are testing. And Andrew Selsky wrote about a fun project in Colombia where people are playing around with different ways to travel without cars. The best thing? Less driving means the air is cleaner, there is less traffic, and the roads are safer.

Cars make a huge mess of pollution. Robert Duffer mentions that in France, "Diesels make up 67 percent of the cars..." (Duffer 16). The reason? The government taxes diesel less. But, diesel hurts the environment more than gas, which makes pollution even worse. Cutting back on these fuels could really clean up the air. Also, cars cause around 12 percent of greenhouse gases in Europe. However, in some parts of the U.S., it is a crazy 50 percent (Rosenthal 5). This really shows how much cars add to pollution in the United States. Luckily, taking trains or carpooling can really help lower this.

More cars just mean more traffic jams. Rosenthal even says, "New York's bikesharing and rising bridge tolls show these new goals. (Rosenthal 37)" The steady stream of cars, for work and everything else, means toll bridges make a ton of money. Carpooling is a great way to use toll bridges less. Sharing rides with people you work with can really make traffic lighter.

Also, public transportation, like the BART in San Francisco, has seriously cut down on the number of drivers. Sure, public transportation can take longer, but it is a much better way to get around. (Rosenthal 40) This makes fewer cars on the road. With fewer cars, it is easier for everyone to travel.

Safety is a big reason to drive less, too. In Colombia, they have a car-free day every year where everyone walks or bikes. (Selsky 20) This means fewer cars and a much smaller chance of car crashes. If we find other ways to travel, there will be fewer accidents. While lots of people need cars, safety is super important. Also, new tech helps people drive less. Telecommunication lets people talk to friends and family from far away. (Rosenthal 35) Because of that, people don't need to travel as much. Texting and social media help people stay connected, meaning less trouble.

So, cars cause lots of problems like accidents, crazy traffic, and awful pollution. By driving less, we can fix these problems and make a safer, healthier place for everyone.

So, the Electoral College works by citizens choosing electors. These electors then cast votes for the President, and Congress finalizes the count. However, the question arises: is this system superior to simply tabulating each individual's vote? This debate is quite common. Despite its long-standing history, the Electoral College can appear somewhat unwieldy. A direct popular vote, on the other hand, would arguably be more equitable and reduce the potential for complete impasses. It is a more logical approach, isn't it?

Ditching the car? Okay, that seems tough, you betcha! Things could get complicated. But hold on a sec! So many wins await you in the wings. Imagine sparkling, fresh air – a total score for all of us! And guess what else? Opting for alternate routes might seriously pad your wallet.

Curbing car use? A major pollution buster, absolutely! Elisabeth Rosenthal points out in part five that cars spew around half the U.S. emissions. That sounds epic, but some cities are already on it. Check out Paris. They were drowning in smog! But by banning specific cars on certain days, poof, the smog vanished fast. Traffic plummeted by a whopping 60 percent! If Paris aced it with such a simple trick, other cities can get in on the clean air game, too.

Driving less does what else? Saves you big bucks, that's what! Cars can suck your bank account dry, oh yes. Plus, Rosenthal notes in paragraph twenty-nine that tolls on bridges and tunnels keep digging into your pocket. But bikes? Dirt cheap! Plus, trails let you pedal for free. Some might think bikes are bonkers, but Bogota, Colombia, is already all in. Selsky shares in paragraph twenty that, once a year, nearly all cars get parked. Millions hop in, rain or shine! See? Anyone can ditch the car.

Bottom line: driving less is packed with perks. Hello, clean air! Goodbye, money woes! Tiny changes in your day make an enormous impact.

We get in our cars daily, but have we really thought about the issues this causes, such as stress and air pollution? Imagine reducing how much we drive. Less stress, increased safety in our neighborhoods, and fresher air could become realities. It's beneficial for our planet and protects families from car-related hazards.

Doesn't everyone agree that traffic feels like a major headache, especially when you're already running behind? If we reduced the number of cars on the road, that would directly translate into less traffic. Andrew Selsky pointed out that this would provide "a fantastic way to lower stress." Who enjoys traffic jams, really? They're frustrating and anger-inducing. Stress overall is a health risk, and driving-related stress definitely takes a toll.

Cars are handy for travel, but let's not forget that they also carry risks! After all, cars are built by people, and people aren't perfect. Some errors might be minor, but others could prove fatal. Countless lives have been lost in car accidents, and even more people have suffered the loss of loved ones. When we cut back on driving, that means fewer accidents, making the world a safer place for our families.

Elisabeth Rosenthal noted that transportation ranks as the second-largest source of pollution in the U.S., only after power plants. Pollution is a major concern that we often overlook. Air pollution is impacting the air we breathe! Power plants and cars are rapidly polluting our air. Simply by driving less, we can achieve cleaner air. It benefits the planet and ensures a healthier future for our children.

Given the many upsides to driving less, we might consider following the examples set by Europe and Colombia. Selsky brought up Colombia's "Day Without Cars," a tradition they've upheld for three consecutive years! Germany boasts a car-free suburb, showcasing a totally different way of living. Paris, too, has introduced car-free days, leading to cleaner air and increased citizen happiness. While cars offer convenience, do they justify all the problems they bring?

Automobiles? You spot them practically everywhere, yeah? Almost can't dodge them! Need to travel? Cars are the go-to. But listen: public transport is roaring back! Seriously cool! It means less smog, fewer traffic jams, and a boost for cities! Cleaner cities used to seem like a pipe dream, but now? It's catching on quick!

Think of cities like Chicago or New York! Remember the bad air? Cars make it worse! Drive less, and boom! Cleaner air and folks are happier! Paris even banned some cars to tackle the crazy pollution. Less car use cuts down on pollution, not just in Paris, but everywhere.

Traffic? Total bummer! Less driving means better, safer roads. Check out Paris after banning cars! Traffic plummeted — a whopping 60%! — despite the smog. Fewer cars also mean fewer accidents! Chicago roads are improving. Less traffic makes roads last longer and easier to fix. Car-Free Day shows this! Picture that across the U.S.! Less driving is a huge win!

Public transport felt unreal. But now? Businesses, even cities, are cutting back on cars big time. Less car activity in crowded areas? Awesome! New sidewalks, cool restaurants popping up—huge perks! Instead of always fixing broken roads, cities can use that money for cool stuff, like parks! More shops and restaurants too! Cities thrive with public transport, relying less on cars.

Cars? Sure, they are convenient. But believe it or not, cutting back on cars is possible with less smog, less traffic, and nicer cities. Quitting cars sounds tough, but things are changing! Big cities are adding public transport to smaller towns. The benefit of less smog outweighs small problems. Smart ideas can ditch cars in the suburbs. It encourages good building and fixes. Smart stuff can cut car use, building a better, safer future!

A lot of people really do not care for driving. And increasingly, countries are limiting how much cars are used, and sometimes stopping it. But you know what? It is possibly a good idea.

Cars put out a lot of really bad gas. Therefore, less driving means less pollution in the air. It is a start to preventing us from ruining our Earth. Even though there is pollution already, small actions make a big difference! Think about saving money - no car bills, and less gas. You can spend that saved money on things you like! We could find other ways to get around.

People who study society also believe that fewer cars help the planet and reduce pollution. As one person put it: "After power plants, cars cause the most pollution in the US!" What it all means: fewer cars = healthier planet = happier people.

Another big thing is money! People could save the money spent on gas, repairs, and buying a car, especially when jobs are not secure. And with that saved income, people can spend it on important things in life!

To be honest, using a car is not always a requirement. For example, teenagers drive to see friends or get to their jobs. Even so, there are other options! I am not saying to stop using cars altogether, but to cut down on unnecessary trips. Walk if you are able to!

Elisabeth Rosenthal, who wrote "The End of Car Culture," pointed out that her kids, ages 19 and 21, did not even bother getting licenses! And they are doing well! She said, "They get to their summer jobs and hang out by walking, taking the bus, or sharing rides with their friends." Sharing rides is doable and reduces traffic, and wasting money.

In general, needing cars less has so many good things. And I think there are EVEN MORE benefits that we are going to find out about!

Perhaps attempting to reduce your driving habits? It is truly a beneficial scenario for both you, as well as our planet!

Honestly, it turns out that it also comes with benefits to you. Imagine eliminating driving as a huge relief. One German family remarked, "Having a car was troublesome. Now we experience a greater sense of liberation!" It extends past merely relieving stress, though. You also conserve funds on gasoline and could improve your physical condition. Considering walking or cycling in place of starting up your car? Think about Bogota’s “Car-Free Day.” Parks, sports zones, better walkways, and interesting stores began showing up seemingly out of nowhere. One study even referred to this as "a possibility to de-stress." It serves as just one advantage of lessening how much we drive.

And let's take into account the broader context. Lowering the amount you drive genuinely impacts the Earth positively. Experts emphasize that cars cause pollution. They stated that "Vehicles generate 12% of Europe’s complete pollution!” Therefore, by logical extension, a reduction in automobiles indicates cleaner air for us all. Paris, a metropolis known for its smog, experimented with rotating license plates. Despite some objections, it brought about less fuel consumption. Columbia even has a yearly car-free day, letting only buses and cabs operate, "to encourage people to find other ways to travel and lower air pollution.” We are all able to contribute! Here in the US, a trend shows fewer individuals are buying cars, driving frequently, or even securing licenses, thereby showing a change regarding the public perception of cars. Some individuals may protest, but the globe reaps the rewards. A population that is happy, a globe that is healthier? These stand as outstanding justifications for driving to a lesser extent.

America votes every four years. Election Day is the Tuesday right after the first Monday in November. But it's more than just choosing a President. Our democracy's Electoral College makes it tricky. Electors pick the President and Vice President, and Congress counts their choices. Political groups usually pick these electors. Is this system really the best? I think we could do better.

We don't directly choose our President. Our votes go to the Electoral College, who then pick electors. They, not us, make the final choice. "What Is the Electoral College?" says that when you vote, you support electors who back a candidate. Almost anyone can be an elector, unless they're in office. Their decision is final. It's not that simple! "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" shows over 60% of people want easier ways to vote. Most crave straightforward voting.

Electoral votes aren't fairly divided. Big states like California get way more votes than small ones like Vermont. The House tries to give everyone a voice, but the Electoral College doesn't. It's unbalanced. This is a big problem because it doesn't match our ideas of fairness.

The Electoral College confuses people. Voters might not know who they're supporting, and they can't control what the electors do. Electors who go against what voters want cause problems. "Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" shows this problem. Voters pick electors, who pick the President, so it's not direct.

Some say getting rid of the Electoral College could hurt our country and go against American values. "In defense of the Electoral College..." suggests parties pick electors who should back their candidate, but the person with the most votes might still lose. Even if you vote, your vote might not matter. It stops runoff elections and helps if no one gets a majority, but there are still issues.

In short, the Electoral College isn't good for our country. It's hard to get and feels unfair. It might have good points, but the bad ones are bigger. It's uneven and could upset our country. For a fairer America, we should get rid of it.

Driving less really helps, you know? Heidrun Walter, a mom, felt way more relaxed when she didn't drive as much. Parking? Totally pricey! Imagine paying $40,000 just to park at your own house! Cars are also super polluters, making up around 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, and even more in the US – sometimes a whole half! If we drive less, we get cool walkable neighborhoods. Plus, clean air is awesome for our planet. Hybrid cars are getting popular because they're cleaner. But weather plays a part; cold nights and hot days trap pollution, which makes the air nasty.

Paris had some seriously bad car smog, so they made rules to fix it quick. If you broke those rules, it cost $31, and they could even take your car away! This cut traffic down big time, like by 60%! The air got better after the rules, so Paris let some cars back in, like cars with odd license plates on Tuesdays. Carlos Arturo says biking with his wife is way less of a headache, and Andrew Selsky noticed the air is cleaner. More cities are digging this idea; Bogota, Colombia, built over 118 miles of bike paths.

Enrique Rivera, the mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, saw other leaders checking out Bogota because they were impressed by the changes and wanted to do the same. By 2013, Americans drove about 9% less, like back in 1995. The economy has something to do with it; less money means fewer cars, and jobless people don't drive as much. If you can't swing a car, a bike seems like a better way to get to work. But some folks just hate biking or walking. And, cars cost a fortune with gas and insurance.

Okay, here's some good news: Americans might be cooling off on cars. As families grew, people actually drove less in 2005. Cars make roads unsafe too. There are fewer young drivers (16-39), and older people are holding onto their licenses longer. Studies showed young people drove 23% less from 2001 to 2009. Bill Ford wants to team up with phone companies to build smart cities where walking, biking, cars, and buses all work together to be efficient, save stuff, pollute less, and be safer. Some spots in Germany banned cars, and now 70% of families don't have cars, and 57% sold their cars before moving there. This helps clean the air and protect the planet, which is a win-win.

Cars are everywhere you look! So many people around the world use them to get around. They sure make life easier by making errands and trips a breeze. But even though they are so convenient, we often don't think about the downsides. Cars help us out, sure, but depending on them too much can cause more trouble than they are worth. Driving too much can really hurt the environment and make us stressed, even if we don't realize it. That's why we should really think about how much we drive.

Our world gives us important things like food and the air we breathe; however, cars cause problems. They release carbon dioxide, which pollutes the air. Robert Duff wrote an article called "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," and in it, he mentions that Paris had such bad air pollution that they had to stop people from driving. This was because lots of people were driving diesel cars because they got tax breaks. Duff points out that "67 percent of cars in France are diesels, compared to only a.3 percent in other Western European countries, according to Reuters." Duff also says that bigger cities usually have worse air. Because cars hurt the environment, people are trying to use them less. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," and she tells the story of Vauban, Germany, where people don't own cars. In Vauban, 70% of families don't have cars, and 57% sell their cars when they move there. People can still have cars, but parking is super expensive, costing around 40,000. Instead, people walk or bike. Vauban shows us that it is possible to cut down on pollution from cars.

Besides hurting the environment, cars also make us stressed without us even knowing it. Heidrun Walter said that she is happier without a car in "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars": "I was always stressed when I had a car. Now I am much happier." Likewise, a businessman named Carlos Arturo said that a car-free day in Bogota was "a good way to reduce stress and clean up the air." Cars can take over our lives, and we might not realize it until we start using them less.

Folks just ain't as car-crazy as they used to be, ya know? Back in the day, a car was *the* way to get around, but with all this fancy tech, fewer people are hittin' the road, especially in the big cities. You might think less driving is a bummer, but hold on! It's actually pretty sweet in a bunch of ways!

The good ol' USA, we always loved our cars! We gave the world the Model T, and who can forget "Mustang Sally?" Even Obama wanted us to lay off the gas pedal a bit to help Mother Earth. He talked about how things were changing big time in "The End of Car Culture." Studies are showin' that folks are buyin' fewer cars, drivin' less, and not even botherin' with a license as much. By 2013, we were drivin' almost 9% less than before! And fewer young'uns are gettin' their licenses. Chalk it up to the internet, lettin' us work from home, cities gettin' spiffier, and those handy-dandy carpool apps on our phones. Cars ain't totally useless, mind you, but life's easier than in the sixties thanks to tech. Here in the U.S., drivin's more of a choice, but in places like Paris, France, they got some rules about it.

Paris, now that's a beautiful city, but it's got a nasty pollution problem, see? Headlines were blarin' "Paris bans driving due to smog!" They stopped some folks from driving to clean up the air. If you drive when you ain't supposed to, you gotta cough up 22 euros. Only cars with certain plates can drive on certain days. The article also said that traffic dropped by a whopping 60% after they did that! Some cars could still roll, like hybrids and cars with three or more people crammed inside. Diesel cars, super popular in France, were the big bad polluters. Regular gas cars weren't as much of a problem. Just like Paris, Bogota, Colombia is tryin' to clean up its act too.

Colombia's got a car-free day where drivin's a no-go! It's a full 24-hour ban, stricter than what Paris does. Drive, and you'll be payin' 25. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" said that "millions of Colombians walk, bike, skate, or take buses to work during the carfree day." Just like Paris, Bogota wants folks to find other ways to get around and cut down on pollution. A businessman named Carlos Arturo Plaza said it's "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Drivin' can be a real headache, especially with road rage, which can ruin your whole day. Drivin's okay sometimes, sure, but there are other ways. Colombia wants to "promote alternative transportation and reduce smog." Cars are gettin' fancier, but people ain't as pumped about 'em anymore, even though they used to be all the rage. Now, they're mostly for gettin' from A to B when you're out of options.

One major upside to not driving as much? Without a doubt, it’s cleaner air! Urban areas suffocate under car fumes, particularly when traffic is heavy. Think of Paris – occasionally, they restrict cars due to extreme air pollution. This air contamination exacerbates climate change and impairs our respiratory health. When Paris faced severe smog, banning cars led to a dramatic 60% traffic decrease! Weather can also intensify pollution; cold nights with warm days can worsen the issue.

Furthermore, ditching the car encourages activity! Individuals tend to walk or bike more, enhancing physical fitness. Opportunities arise to socialize and enjoy the outdoors. As pedestrian activity increases, gyms and parks thrive, and new shops and eateries emerge, stimulating local economies.

Increasingly, people explore alternative transportation like buses, bikes, or walking. Research indicates a decline in young drivers – a significant 23% decrease between 2001 and 2009. Wider adoption of ride-sharing or other transportation modes would reduce pollution levels universally. Reduced car emissions would invariably improve air quality and facilitate easier breathing.

Consider that resources allocated to roads and traffic could be re-directed to superior investments if dependence on cars diminished. While driving is prevalent, alternatives exist. Reducing driving translates to financial savings, especially with fluctuating gas prices.

In summary, numerous compelling reasons exist to reduce or eliminate car usage. Beyond environmental benefits like cleaner air, personal health improves. Walking, biking, or using public transport offers economical mobility! Moreover, savings accrue on fuel, vehicle maintenance, and car payments.

Alright, so we Americans are big fans of cars, right? But have you given any thought to other travel options, like, ever? You know what? Seriously, letting go of the car is surprisingly amazing! You'll find so many unexpected benefits if you do.

Imagine a world without cars! Wouldn't it be fantastic if shops were just around the corner? Forget about driving to those enormous malls miles away! I mean, just a quick walk down the street. Homes, shops, and workplaces all bundled together, so walkable!

Walking to stores? A complete game-changer! It's obvious: less driving. Furthermore, if everyone drives less, what happens? Yup, more walking and biking! Hello, better health! And science backs it up: a daily walk is great for your mind.

Oh, and definitely, less driving means cleaner air, duh. Cars spew out nasty pollution that turns into smog, gross! So, drive less? You get less stress and cleaner air! Talk about a win-win!

Here's something big: cars are massive polluters! It's already a huge 12% of greenhouse gasses in Europe. But guess what? It's an insane 50% in the US! Seriously, cutting down on driving will help the planet a lot.

What's the deal? There are other ways to travel! We can pick options that are great for our bodies and the earth. So, with all these amazing benefits to driving less, how about giving it a shot? You'll thank yourself later.

Imagine a world with no cars. It sounds unreal, but these ideas are happening now! New towns pop up, some cities restrict cars, and countries hold car-free days. People often forget that cars make a lot of pollution. We must act now and think about the damage!

Take Vauban, Germany, for instance; it's incredible! To live there, you either ditch your car or pay a hefty $40,000 parking fee! Guess what the study found? "70% of Vauban families don't own cars, and 57% sold theirs to move there." One mom, Heidrun Walter, said, "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I'm much happier!" This is "Smart Planning," which means designing areas where you don't need cars. Cars create 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, and a huge 50% in the U.S. Smart Planning can cut these gases and give us cleaner air!

Paris, France, shows just how bad car pollution can get. One source said, "After heavy pollution, Paris restricted some cars to clean the air." This slashed traffic by 60% in just five days. Diesel fuel, used by 67% of French vehicles, was the main cause of smog. The weather didn't help; "cold nights and warm days trapped car emissions," or so it was said.

But it's not just Europe. Bogota, Colombia, held its third "Day Without Cars," only allowing buses and taxis. Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "It's a good chance to reduce stress and pollution." Plus, Cali and Valledupar, two other Colombian cities, had their first Day Without Cars this year!

Even in the U.S., car use is changing. Believe it or not, miles driven hit their peak in 2005 and have been falling ever since. One source claims, "In April 2013, miles driven per person were almost 9% lower than the peak, like going back to January 1995." Less car use is great for the planet but not so great for car companies. Ford and Mercedes are turning into "mobility" companies. New York City has bike-sharing and new bike paths. Even tunnel tolls show this change.

Cars have harmed our planet, and we have to fix it. With these amazing, eco-friendly ideas, we're moving towards a future where cars don't control us.

Honestly, the Electoral College remains quite perplexing. While its establishment traces back quite far, it begs the question: are its initial justifications still relevant? In a time when political acumen was less widespread, informed voting presented a challenge. Today, a deluge of news and candidate information surrounds us. Wouldn't a system based purely on the popular vote prove more democratic in electing the president? The president should be accountable to the entire populace, not merely a select few influential figures.

That said, even proponents acknowledge flaws within the Electoral College framework. While we believe we are voting for the President, we are truly voting for electors. This decision is not ours, but the state's. States choose electors through various methods. These electors pledge allegiance to a specific candidate, then cast their votes. However, Bradford Plumer points out the inherent uncontrollability of electors. Moreover, he suggests that voters, confused by the process, may inadvertently miscast their votes. Instances of "faithless electors," who betray their pledges, further complicate matters!

The Electoral College system creates a disproportionate emphasis on "swing states." Those states where either candidate can win become extremely important. Because the winner receives all of a state's votes, those who vote in states that usually support one side or the other feel as if their vote doesn't matter and therefore don't vote. This causes campaigns to ignore the majority of the country's voters. Should we have a popular vote, candidates would then have to try and talk to everyone and their issues.

Choosing a president through a popular vote appears undeniably more equitable. A vote for the popular vote ensures even smaller groups within traditionally aligned states will feel more represented. Polls indicate overwhelming voter preference for direct presidential selection. Even supporters of the Electoral College acknowledge its potential for causing ties, while also stating that any one vote lacks significance— a statement that, by itself, lacks significance! That's absurd! Such notions dampen voter enthusiasm and undermine the integrity of elections. Every single vote holds value, and equitable representation is every citizen's right. A popular vote stands as the most effective means of electing a president who represents ALL Americans.

After the Second World War, cars became super popular! Articles 2 and 3 looked at cities trying out days without cars, but some folks in articles 1 and 4 said they weren't driving as much. When people drive less, there's less pollution and it's easier to get around!

Pollution from the air is a big issue. Rosenthal pointed out that "cars are responsible for 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe... and in some spots in the U.S., it's as high as 50% P5!" All those cars lead to nasty smog in cities. Remember the 2009 Beijing Olympics? When they cut down on cars, the air got way better! This was super important to keep the athletes and tourists healthy, especially since everyone in Beijing was already wearing masks. It's not easy, but cutting back on car use stops bad stuff from happening. Duffer mentions that Paris has really brought down pollution, even banning certain license plates P19! By controlling car pollution, we get cleaner air and can see better!

What's interesting is that lots of young people are skipping getting their driver's licenses, even though they grew up around cars. Selsky says that fewer cars mean more room for parks, sports fields, stores, and places just for walking P28! Plus, it's great for new shops and restaurants! Instead of malls next to highways, shopping spots are popping up near trains and buses, where people can easily walk around. Rosenthal also talks about how his family started using buses, carpools, and their feet more, and driving less P40. Taking the bus or train is now the smart way to go! And carpooling is also a big help. With carpools, walking, and buses, getting around and seeing the city has become way easier.

Cities are changing to make things better for people who don't use cars. When we don't use cars so much, there are lots of good things that happen. Cleaner air and easier ways to get around are making cars less important. If we keep reducing how much we use cars, life could get even better in the future!

Alright, so the electoral college works like this: folks are chosen as electors, they cast votes for the president, and then they count those votes up. But seriously, is this a smart way to run things, or is it just a ticking time bomb? Honestly, it needs to go! It's not a direct popular vote, and that can cause a lot of issues.

Some folks say the electoral college stops messy runoff elections, which could mean things run more smoothly. Richard A. Posner pointed out that in super-tight elections, people actually *want* runoffs. So, keeping the electoral college, they say, makes runoffs less likely. But is that really the best fix, and what problems could it cause? Since electors pick the president, can we even count on them to stop runoffs? Posner thinks it does lower the urge to mess things up further. Even so, that one small plus isn't worth all the major downsides.

Just picture if everything goes totally wrong. We'd be in deep trouble! The author says we lucked out that the 2000 election was only the worst in a hundred years; things could definitely be much worse. It's happened before too. Plumer believes the electoral college has seriously damaged the U.S., and keeping it could lead to even more chaos. The author reminds us that electors have, in the past, voted against their party, according to Plumer. So, it's not just pointless, but also easily rigged. It should be trashed, since it could wreck our government.

Plus, it's not a direct vote by the people. The Office of the Federal Register says when you vote for a candidate, you're actually voting for *their* electors. So, you're not directly picking who runs the country. It feels wrong for leaders to make big decisions for the people when they didn't even vote for them. Plumer calls it unfair, old-fashioned, and just plain dumb. These problems prove the electoral college hurts faith in the government. Plumer also notes that candidates just skip over states they don't think they can win, and only focus on "swing" states. Winning is more important than what's actually best for the country. Are we really going to let this thing ruin us?

Bottom line, the electoral college? Ditch it.

Dear Florida Senator, The people who started our country set up electors so the president would be the people's choice. Yet, this system means the person with the most votes might not win. This outdated setup raises the risk of a tie. A lot of Americans crave true democracy, but this system can ruin it.

The popular vote is the best way to see what the country wants. A huge amount, "over 60 percent," want to vote directly for the president. The people want their vote to count. When the President sends soldiers to war, citizens want their voices to be heard, and their votes should matter, not be ignored by the electoral college. Only 538 electors have way more power than millions of regular voters. We have rights like "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," so freedom must include fair voting.

The electoral college has been around for ages. But this is also why it's a problem. To stay current, the U.S. must update the voting rules. As the world is changing, the system must change too. We should be thankful that "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century" because it might happen again.

Even worse, we might end up with a tie, messing things up even more. It was mentioned that "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Democracy should not end in ties. The people's vote, not a stalemate, should be what decides elections. Source 2 says, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." The popular vote is useless if there's a tie, which is frightening.

In conclusion, the electoral college should be gotten rid of as it hurts the people. It makes our votes less important, is old, and could lead to a tie. I hope we select the president based on what the people want, not the views of those in D.C.

Alright, so think about cars. They’re how you get around - doing tasks, meeting friends. How about if they disappeared? Strange, right? But, surprising as it sounds, some genuinely positive outcomes could occur.

Firstly, a big win for our planet! Cars pollute massively, don’t they? Consider this: "Passenger cars? They cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases!" Furthermore, "Up to half in areas of US with lots of cars!" (That's from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). Ergo, no cars, cleaner air, and no nasty smog. The air would be so much healthier!

Additionally, we’d be healthier too. Cleaner air, certainly! Additionally, without cars, walking or biking happens. "Vauban," as "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, mentions, "with its 5,500 folks, is car-free." Shops are near, "easy to walk to," instead of "miles away." So, people walk, bike, or use buses. More activity, less couch time. Wonderful!

Moreover, cars are maybe declining. "Obama's push to cut US emissions," says "The End of Car Culture," paragraph 29, "is helped by fewer car buyers, less driving, and fewer licenses." Gas is pricey, texting while driving is dangerous, and new drivers can be clueless. People seem less fond of cars. Great for the planet, no? Maybe driving will totally vanish one day.

Therefore, fewer cars? That's a total victory! The planet breathes better, and people already ditch cars. Why burn gas when you can walk, bike, and stay fit? Less driving? Fantastic!

The Electoral College, an old system, makes people really mad and sparks lots of arguments. Should we get rid of it, or keep it as is? Some say it's totally unfair and hurts both voters and those running for president. Others think it's the best way to pick a president. Both sides have good points, so what, if anything, should we change?

Instead of just counting votes, the Electoral College tries to make things fair in presidential elections. Each state's political groups suggest a group of electors. So, when you vote, you're really backing the electors your candidate picked.

So, why do some folks want to ditch the Electoral College? Well, things can get messy. One big worry is that things could get stuck. How could that happen? Electors could change their minds and vote how they personally feel. Even though you think your vote picks electors, they might not always do what you expect. This is a huge problem if electors bail on their party's choice. After Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000 but didn't win the election, lots of voters wanted direct elections. Many voters really hate the Electoral College.

But, some folks are really big fans and want to keep it. Each party picks electors who will likely support their candidate, and usually, they do. The party tends to pick people who will stick by their candidate. It also shows how important it is to get support from different parts of the country. A president who only wins in one small area might not be a good leader for everyone. They can't help everyone if they only care about one place. Because of this, they probably won't win. Also, the Electoral College can help when no one gets a clear majority.

When we think about what to do with the Electoral College – keep it or scrap it – there are strong reasons on both sides. In the end, it's up to the people to decide.

For families needing to cart their children to school and other activities, cars are viewed as indispensable. However, a growing number of people are starting to wonder if perhaps driving less could alleviate some of the environmental issues we currently face.

The environmental impact of cars is widely known. Key concerns include air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions, which contribute significantly to global warming. While cars account for about 12% of these detrimental emissions in Europe, the figure can soar to as high as 50% in the US, where driving is a ubiquitous practice. Furthermore, heavy traffic exacerbates the situation. In France, for example, diesel vehicles intensify urban smog, particularly during periods of high temperatures. Therefore, reducing driving leads to cleaner air for everyone.

In addition to environmental benefits, opting out of car use can enhance personal well-being. Many individuals worldwide have experimented with car-free living, embracing alternative modes of transportation. Heidrun Walkter, a financially comfortable German mother, relinquished her car and now enjoys walking and the sounds of bikes and children. She states, "I was constantly stressed with a car. Now, I experience greater happiness." Moreover, reduced driving encourages increased physical activity. In Colombia, millions participated in a car-free day, choosing walking, cycling, or public transportation to combat smog. The initiative proved successful, fostering a sense of community and reducing stress. One man, cycling with his wife, remarked that it was beneficial for both the Earth and their own well-being.

In conclusion, lessening our reliance on cars offers a straightforward method for improving air quality, reducing smog, and promoting cycling. From Germany and France to Colombia and the US, numerous nations are actively seeking solutions to mitigate the adverse effects associated with driving.

Senator, do support the proposal to choose Presidents via popular vote! It is of utmost importance, and here is why. Electors could go rogue, ignoring the public's desires. Furthermore, a deadlock could occur, leading to a House decision. Additionally, the winner-take-all system has flaws.

Electors are not dependable, and they might falter again. Consider Bradford Plumer's point: some nearly derailed the 1960 election by altering electors. They occasionally vote as they please, even against their own party!

A deadlock in the Electoral College is a frightful scenario. Then, the House makes the call, with each state receiving a single vote. Incredibly, tiny Wyoming's influence matches that of massive California! In 1976, subtle vote shifts in Ohio and Hawaii could have caused a tie. That was uncomfortably close!

The winner-take-all arrangement is unfair to voters. Candidates concentrate on swing states, neglecting other citizens. In 2000, numerous states were ignored by candidates. Many urban voters did not encounter a single ad. How can we permit voters in states like Ohio to elect the President? It seems unjustifiable.

So, Senator, heed this call: advocate a popular vote system for selecting Presidents! Electors may defy voters, a tie sends it to the House, and winner-take-all is unjust and improper for America.
